@graph.name: BMW X5 xDrive25d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 xDrive25d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X5 xDrive25d engine typically costs £1,500–£5,500 supply only depending on which generation (N47 or B47), with used units available from £1,000–£2,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 xDrive25d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which generation. The N47 (2013–2015) has a well-documented timing chain weakness — the rear-mounted chain is labour-intensive to replace. The B47 (2015+) moved the chain to the front and is significantly more reliable. Both engines can suffer from DPF clogging and oil dilution. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 xDrive25d?

acceptedAnswer.text: N47: timing chain wear (rear-mounted design), DPF clogging, oil dilution from DPF regeneration. B47: DPF clogging, oil dilution, occasional turbocharger wear. The B47 is significantly more reliable than the N47.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 xDrive25d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept G05 X5 xDrive25d worth £25,000–£40,000+, a £2,500–£5,500 recon is only 10–20% — well within the 70% BMW threshold. Against a tired F15 X5 with an N47 worth £6,000–£12,000, a £1,500–£3,500 recon is 30–50% — borderline, so evaluate carefully.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 xDrive25d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. F15 X5 (2013–2015) uses N47D20; F15 X5 (2015–2018) uses B47D20; G05 X5 (2018+) uses B47D20B. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N47 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It needs replacement — the chain stretches and guides wear. Replacement costs £1,000–£2,500 and is labour-intensive due to the rear-mounted chain location. A preventative replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£1,500+). At the first sign of cold-start rattle on an N47, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The xDrive25d THROUGH TIME — Era Map

replacementCosts.h2: BMW X5 xDrive25d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current X5 xDrive25d market. N47-engined cars sit at the lower end; B47 examples command a premium. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X5 xDrive25d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The N47's rear-mounted chain location makes timing chain replacement particularly labour-intensive.

cta.label: → Compare xDrive25d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Failure (Rear-Mounted Design)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 2–5 seconds, metallic ticking from the rear of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors, eventual chain slip
problems.replacementCost: £1,500–£3,500 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any cold-start rattle on an N47 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: The N47's timing chain is mounted at the rear of the engine — a design that makes replacement exceptionally labour-intensive (engine-out job). The chain can stretch and the plastic guides can break, leading to catastrophic engine failure. BMW faced widespread complaints and class action lawsuits over this issue. If you're buying an N47-engined X5 xDrive25d and there's no proof of timing chain work, budget for it immediately — or walk away. Frequent oil changes (every 5,000–8,000 miles) can help prevent chain wear.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: DPF Clogging and Regeneration Issues (N47 and B47)
problems.symptoms: Reduced engine power, increased fuel consumption, warning lights on the dashboard, rough idle, engine hesitation
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the X5 xDrive25d suffers from DPF clogging — particularly if the vehicle is frequently driven on short trips or in urban environments where regeneration cycles are less effective. The 2.0-litre engine works hard to move the heavy X5, which can exacerbate DPF issues. Regular long drives that allow for complete DPF regeneration can help maintain the system. If you see warning lights or notice reduced power, have the DPF system inspected promptly. Prevention is cheaper than replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF failure guide

problems.title: Oil Dilution (Diesel in Oil) — N47 and B47
problems.symptoms: Rising oil level on the dipstick, smell of diesel in the oil, engine warning light, DPF regeneration occurring frequently
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — exacerbated by short journeys
problems.repairCost: £150–£300 (oil and filter change with correct spec oil)
problems.recommendation: Diesel engines suffer from oil dilution — fuel gets past the piston rings during DPF regeneration cycles. Short journeys make this worse. Check the oil level regularly; if it's rising, get an oil change immediately using the correct BMW-spec oil (5W-30 LL-04). Extended dilution damages bearings and leads to catastrophic failure. Regular motorway runs to allow passive DPF regeneration are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil dilution guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: X5 xDrive25d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive (engine-out) but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Forced regeneration or cleaning — prevention is key
Oil change with correct spec — cheap and effective
Turbo replacement — catch before debris damages the engine

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 xDrive25d? Check These First
buyIf: * B47-engined car (late 2015+) — the more reliable engine with the front-mounted chain
* N47-engined car with documented timing chain work — evidence of the upgrade is essential
* Full service history with correct BMW-spec oil (5W-30 LL-04 or 0W-30)
* Evidence of regular motorway driving — DPF regeneration requires motorway runs
avoidIf: * N47-engined car with no proof of timing chain work — you're buying a known risk
* Any cold-start rattle — this is almost certainly timing chain wear on N47
* Evidence of rising oil levels — oil dilution is damaging bearings
* Poor service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X5 xDrive25d is a capable and efficient entry into X5 ownership — but the engine choice matters enormously. The N47 (2013–2015) has a well-documented timing chain weakness — the rear-mounted design makes replacement expensive. The B47 (2015+) relocated the chain to the front, eliminating the most serious flaw. The B47 is the much safer bet. If you're buying an N47, demand timing chain history — or walk away.

cta.label: → Compare xDrive25d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 xDrive25d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain warning on N47)
2. Service History — including which generation (F15 or G05) and evidence of timing chain work on N47
3. Oil Level — Is the oil level rising on the dipstick? (oil dilution warning)
4. DPF Health — Any warning lights or reduced power? (DPF clogging is common)
5. Oil Condition — Any signs of diesel contamination? (fuel in oil)
6. Turbo Performance — Test acceleration; any whistling, hesitation, or loss of power?
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, NOx sensor issues)
